content:
# Leviticus Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71v–72r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Speak to the Israelites and say to them: These are the LORD's appointed times — the sacred assemblies you are to proclaim. These are my appointed times.

**3** "There are six days for ordinary work, but the seventh day is a Sabbath of complete rest — a sacred assembly. You must not do any regular work on it. It is a Sabbath belonging to the LORD, wherever you live.

**4** "These are the LORD's appointed times, the sacred assemblies you are to proclaim at their appointed seasons:

**5** "The LORD's Passover begins at twilight on the fourteenth day of the first month. **6** On the fifteenth day of that same month is the LORD's Festival of Unleavened Bread. For seven days you must eat bread made without yeast. **7** On the first day you are to hold a sacred assembly and do no regular work. **8** For seven days you are to present a food offering to the LORD. On the seventh day there is another sacred assembly, and you must do no regular work."

**9** The LORD spoke to Moses: **10** "Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When you enter the land I am going to give you and you harvest its grain, bring the priest the very first sheaf of your harvest. **11** He is to wave it before the LORD so it will be accepted on your behalf. The priest is to wave it on the day after the Sabbath. **12** On the day you wave the sheaf, you must sacrifice as a burnt offering to the LORD a lamb a year old, without defect, **13** along with its grain offering — two-tenths of an ephah of the finest flour mixed with oil — a food offering presented to the LORD, a pleasing aroma — and a drink offering of a quarter of a hin of wine. **14** You must not eat any bread, or roasted or new grain, until the very day you bring this offering to your God. This is to be a lasting ordinance for all the generations to come, wherever you live.

**15** "Count off seven full weeks from the day after the Sabbath, from the day you brought the wave offering of the sheaf. **16** Count off fifty days up to the day after the seventh Sabbath, and then present an offering of new grain to the LORD. **17** From wherever you live, bring two loaves of bread made of two-tenths of an ephah of the finest flour, baked with yeast, as a wave offering of firstfruits to the LORD. **18** Present with this bread seven lambs without defect, each a year old, one young bull, and two rams. They will be a burnt offering to the LORD, together with their grain offerings and drink offerings — a food offering, an aroma pleasing to the LORD. **19** Then sacrifice one male goat as a sin offering and two lambs, each a year old, as a fellowship offering. **20** The priest is to wave the two lambs before the LORD as a wave offering, together with the bread of the firstfruits. They are sacred to the LORD for the priest. **21** On that same day you are to proclaim a sacred assembly and do no regular work. This is to be a lasting ordinance for all the generations to come, wherever you live.

**22** "When you harvest the grain of your land, do not reap all the way to the very edges of your field or gather the gleanings of your harvest. Leave them for the poor and the foreigner. I am the LORD your God."

**23** The LORD spoke to Moses: **24** "Say to the Israelites: On the first day of the seventh month you are to have a day of rest — a sacred assembly signaled by trumpet blasts. **25** You must not do any regular work, and you are to present a food offering to the LORD."

**26** The LORD spoke to Moses: **27** "The tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. Hold a sacred assembly and humble yourselves, and present a food offering to the LORD. **28** Do no work on that day, because it is the Day of Atonement, when atonement is made for you before the LORD your God. **29** Anyone who does not humble themselves on that day must be cut off from their people. **30** I will destroy from among their people anyone who does any work on that day. **31** You shall do no work at all. This is to be a lasting ordinance for all the generations to come, wherever you live. **32** It will be a sabbath of complete rest for you, and you must humble yourselves. From the evening of the ninth day of the month until the following evening, you shall observe your sabbath."

**33** The LORD spoke to Moses: **34** "Say to the Israelites: On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, the LORD's Festival of Tabernacles begins, and it lasts for seven days. **35** The first day is a sacred assembly; do no regular work. **36** For seven days present food offerings to the LORD, and on the eighth day hold a sacred assembly and present a food offering to the LORD. It is the closing assembly — you must do no regular work.

**37** "These are the LORD's appointed times, which you are to proclaim as sacred assemblies for presenting food offerings to the LORD — burnt offerings and grain offerings, sacrifices and drink offerings, each on its proper day — **38** in addition to the LORD's Sabbaths and in addition to your gifts and all your vow offerings and all the freewill offerings you give to the LORD.

**39** "So beginning with the fifteenth day of the seventh month, after you have gathered the crops of the land, celebrate the festival of the LORD for seven days. The first day is a day of rest, and the eighth day also is a day of rest. **40** On the first day take for yourselves branches from splendid trees — palm fronds, leafy boughs, and willows of the brook — and rejoice before the LORD your God for seven days. **41** Celebrate this as a festival to the LORD for seven days each year. This is to be a lasting ordinance for all the generations to come; celebrate it in the seventh month. **42** Live in temporary shelters for seven days: all native-born Israelites are to live in these shelters **43** so your descendants will know that I made the Israelites live in temporary shelters when I brought them out of Egypt. I am the LORD your God."

**44** So Moses announced to the Israelites the appointed times of the LORD.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### What Leviticus 23 Is Actually About

Leviticus 23 is the sacred calendar of Israel — a map of the entire year organized around divine encounter. The chapter presents seven appointed times (*mo'adim*), each one a moment when Israel is summoned to meet with God. The Hebrew word *mo'ed* (appointment, set time) appears eleven times in this chapter alone. These are not merely religious holidays; they are scheduled meetings. God is the host; Israel is the invited guest; the whole agricultural and liturgical year is structured so that the people's life with the land never runs too far from the presence of the LORD.

The seven appointed times form a complete theological narrative. Passover recalls the Exodus — the act of liberation that made Israel God's people. The Feast of Unleavened Bread extends and embodies that liberation through the week. The Firstfruits offering consecrates the harvest before it is eaten. Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost) celebrates the full first harvest fifty days later. The Feast of Trumpets marks the new year of the seventh month with solemnity. The Day of Atonement confronts Israel's failure — the sin that has accumulated through the year — with the possibility of radical forgiveness. And the Festival of Tabernacles closes the year with a week-long camping trip, a national reenactment of the wilderness journey, a communal act of memory and gratitude.

The structure is not incidental. The calendar begins with liberation (Passover) and ends with memory (Tabernacles). In between, it moves through harvest, covenant, judgment, and atonement. This is not a list of obligations; it is a shaped journey through the full range of human experience before God.

### Verses 1–3: The Sabbath as the Foundation of All Time

Before the annual festivals are listed, the Sabbath is placed first. This positioning is deliberate and profound. The Sabbath is not one festival among others; it is the foundation of all sacred time. The weekly rest shapes the rhythm within which all the annual appointed times occur. God's first gift to Israel's calendar is not a feast but a stop — a regular interruption of ordinary work, a declaration that human life is not defined by productivity.

The language is striking: *shabbat shabbaton* — a "Sabbath of Sabbaths," rendered here as "complete rest." The doubling intensifies the noun; it is a true, full rest, not merely a reduced workday. The Sabbath belongs to the LORD *be-khol moshevoteikhem* — "in all your dwelling places." Wherever Israel lives — in the land, in exile, in diaspora — the Sabbath follows. It is not geographically bound. It is the portable sacred time that constitutes Israel as Israel wherever they find themselves.

The threefold structure of the Sabbath command here — rest, sacred assembly, no work — sets the template for every festival that follows. Each of the great appointed times is essentially a Sabbath writ large: a structured stopping, a summoning together, a prohibition on ordinary labor. The Sabbath is the seed of the entire sacred year.

### Verses 4–8: Passover and Unleavened Bread — The Liberation Liturgy

The calendar opens with Passover on the fourteenth of the first month at twilight. The timing is precise: *bein ha-arbayim*, "between the two evenings," the threshold moment when the day has officially ended but darkness has not yet fully arrived. This is the exact moment Israel sacrificed the lamb in Egypt (Exod 12:6), and the precision is memorial — the appointment is not at Passover-time; it is at the specific minute of the original event.

Passover (*Pesach*) is not defined here by detailed ritual (that was laid out in Exodus 12 and Numbers 9) but by its place in the calendar. It is the anchor. Everything else hangs from it. When the text says these are the LORD's *mo'adim* and immediately launches into Passover, the message is clear: Israel's sacred time is ordered by the memory of its liberation.

The seven days of Unleavened Bread (*Chag HaMatzot*) begin on the fifteenth — the day after Passover — and extend through the week. The leaven-free bread (*matzah*) is memory made edible. Every bite of flat bread during this week activates the body's memory of the hasty departure from Egypt, when there was no time to let bread rise (Exod 12:39). The festival is not nostalgic — it is embodied and active. You do not merely remember the Exodus; you eat its textures for a week.

The sacred assemblies on the first and seventh days, with no regular work, bracket the festival. The first day opens with summoning; the seventh day closes with summoning. The entire week lives within the frame of sacred time.

### Verses 9–14: The Firstfruits Sheaf — Consecrating the Harvest

The instructions for bringing the firstfruits sheaf (*omer*) open with a geographical condition: "When you enter the land I am going to give you." This offering cannot be performed in the wilderness. It belongs to life in Canaan — to actual agriculture, actual harvest, actual soil. The law is future-oriented; it plants a liturgical obligation in the very act of inheriting the land.

The logic of the firstfruits offering is one of the most elegant in the sacrificial system: you bring the very first portion of the harvest to the priest before anyone eats any of the new grain. The priest waves it before the LORD. Only after this act of consecration may the new harvest enter ordinary consumption. The offering does not ask God to bless the harvest retroactively; it consecrates the harvest at its beginning, placing the entire yield within the logic of gift rather than possession.

The Hebrew word *omer* (sheaf, a dry measure) gives its name to the fifty-day counting period between Passover and Shavuot — the *Sefirat HaOmer*, the Counting of the Omer. These seven weeks of counting are themselves an act of liturgical attention: the community tracks time deliberately, day by day, from the wave offering of the first sheaf until the presentation of the new loaves at Shavuot. The counting turns the harvest season into an act of sustained anticipation.

Verse 14 extends the consecration to the entire household of consumption: no bread, no roasted grain, no new grain may be eaten until the wave offering is made. The restriction is comprehensive — it covers the processed (bread), the raw-cooked (roasted grain), and the unprocessed (new grain). Every form of the new harvest waits for consecration. This is not privation; it is an ordering of desire and gratitude. Before you eat, you give.

### Verses 15–22: Shavuot — The Festival of the Covenant

The instruction to "count off seven full weeks" from the Firstfruits offering is the origin of the Jewish festival of Shavuot (*Feast of Weeks*) and the Christian festival of Pentecost. The Greek word *pentecost* simply means "fiftieth" — the fiftieth day after the Firstfruits wave offering. The counting is mandatory: *seven complete weeks*, *fifty days*. The precision is not bureaucratic; it is theological. The gift of the harvest is arriving in measured, anticipated time.

The Shavuot offering has a striking feature that sets it apart from almost every other sacrifice in Leviticus: the two loaves of firstfruits bread are baked **with yeast** (v.17). Leaven is normally excluded from offerings (Lev 2:11). Why is it permitted — required — here? Because these loaves are not burned on the altar; they are brought before the LORD and then given to the priests. They represent real food — the actual bread of the community's table, made the way ordinary people make bread. They are not a pure altar offering; they are the firstfruits of the community's daily life, brought as they are, leavened and all, before the LORD.

The comprehensive sacrificial package of verses 18–19 — seven lambs, a bull, two rams, a male goat, and two lambs again — is one of the richest offerings in the festival calendar. The grain offerings and drink offerings accompany everything. The sin offering (*chattat*, a male goat) reminds the community that even a festival of firstfruits and gratitude must pass through the acknowledgment of sin. Celebration does not bypass atonement; it includes it.

The gleaning law of verse 22 interrupts the festival calendar unexpectedly. Nestled between Shavuot instructions (v.21) and the Feast of Trumpets (v.23), the instruction to leave the corners of fields and the gleanings of the harvest for the poor and the foreigner seems out of place. But its placement is deliberate. The harvest festival is not complete without the provision for those who have no harvest of their own. The celebration of abundance is hollow if it does not extend to the marginalized. The firstfruits offering and the gleaning law belong together: you consecrate what you have by giving the first portion to God, and you sanctify your celebration by leaving the edges for those who have nothing. The gleaning law is the social implementation of firstfruits theology.

### Verses 23–25: The Feast of Trumpets — New Year in the Seventh Month

The seventh month is the most sacred month in the Hebrew calendar, containing three of the seven appointed times (Trumpets, Atonement, and Tabernacles) within a span of twenty-two days. It opens on the first day with the blast of the *shofar* — the ram's horn that sounds the arrival of sacred time.

The text is spare here: a sacred assembly, trumpet blasts, no regular work, a food offering. The festival is called in rabbinic tradition *Rosh Hashanah* (New Year's Head), though the Pentateuch never uses this term. The month is the seventh in the religious calendar (which begins with Nisan in the spring) but the first in the civil/agricultural calendar. The tension between these two orderings — sacred time beginning in spring with Passover, civic and agricultural time beginning in fall — gives this day its liminal character.

The shofar blast (*teruah*) is the announcement. In the ancient Near East, the shofar was blown to sound an alarm, to call an assembly, to announce the arrival of a king. On the first of the seventh month, it summons Israel to sacred attention. Ten days remain until the Day of Atonement. The trumpet begins the period of introspection and preparation. The new year does not begin with celebration alone; it begins with a summons to awareness.

### Verses 26–32: The Day of Atonement — The Heart of the Sacred Year

The Day of Atonement (*Yom Kippur*) is the climax of Leviticus's entire sacrificial and purification theology. The full ritual has been described in chapter 16; here its place in the calendar is secured and its demands defined: the tenth of the seventh month, a sacred assembly, *inui nefesh* (self-affliction, self-humbling — fasting in practice, though the text does not use the word "fast"), no work of any kind.

The phrase *ve-innitem et nafshoteikhem* — "you shall humble/afflict your souls" — appears three times in these verses (vv.27, 29, 32). The repetition is not redundancy; it is emphasis. The Day of Atonement demands a particular posture of the inner person. The word *inui* comes from the root *'anah* — to be bowed down, to be humbled, to be afflicted. It is the same root used when the Egyptians "afflicted" Israel in slavery (Exod 1:11–12). On Yom Kippur, Israel voluntarily takes the posture of affliction — not as victims but as penitents. They bow under the weight of their own sin rather than under the cruelty of a taskmaster.

The dual threat of verses 29–30 is extraordinary in its intensity. Those who do not humble themselves will be "cut off from their people" (v.29); those who do any work at all, God himself will "destroy" (*ve-hid'iti*). This is the harshest consequence in the festival calendar. No other feast carries this double threat. The Day of Atonement is not optional, not adjustable, not private. It is the day when the entire community is equally and simultaneously before God in their failure — and to refuse that posture is to step outside the covenant community altogether.

Verse 32 defines the day's boundaries with precision: from the evening of the ninth until the evening of the tenth. The Hebrew day runs from sunset to sunset. Yom Kippur begins at sundown on the ninth and ends at sundown on the tenth — a full twenty-five hour period in rabbinic reckoning. The chapter calls it *shabbat shabbaton* — the Sabbath of Sabbaths, the same doubling used for the weekly Sabbath in verse 3. Of all the appointed times, only the weekly Sabbath and Yom Kippur receive this title. Their kinship is deep: both are acts of total stopping, complete cessation, radical rest. But where the Sabbath is rest from labor, Yom Kippur is rest from self — a day-long relinquishment of the ordinary drives of eating, working, self-maintenance, so that the soul can face what it has become.

### Verses 33–43: The Festival of Tabernacles — Memory, Joy, and Shelter

The Festival of Tabernacles (*Chag HaSukkot*) is the great closing feast — seven days beginning on the fifteenth of the seventh month, followed by an eighth day that is itself a sacred closing assembly (*atzeret*). The character of this festival is unmistakably joyful. The detailed prescription of verse 40 captures something of its physical exuberance: "take for yourselves branches from splendid trees — palm fronds, leafy boughs, and willows of the brook — and rejoice before the LORD your God."

The four species of verse 40 became in Jewish practice the *arba'ah minim* (four species) of Sukkot: the *etrog* (citron, the "fruit of splendid trees"), the *lulav* (palm frond), the *hadassim* (myrtle branches), and *aravot* (willow branches). These are bundled together and waved in all six directions — north, south, east, west, up, down — as a declaration that the LORD is present and sovereign everywhere. The physical gesture of Sukkot wave-offering is one of the most embodied acts in the entire liturgical calendar.

But the festival's deepest meaning is in the *sukkah* — the temporary shelter. For seven days, all native-born Israelites are to live in these booths (v.42). The reason is stated with startling directness in verse 43: "so your descendants will know that I made the Israelites live in temporary shelters when I brought them out of Egypt."

The sukkah is a memory device. It is forty years of wilderness wandering reduced to seven days. When a family moves out of their permanent home and into a fragile, leafy booth, they are enacting the vulnerability of the wilderness — the radical dependence on God that defined the Exodus generation. The flimsy walls of the sukkah are a theological statement: our security does not come from our architecture. The permanent house is an illusion of self-sufficiency. The temporary shelter tells the truth: we are pilgrims; we have always been pilgrims; our permanence is God, not our buildings.

The eighth day, the *Shemini Atzeret* (Solemn Assembly of the Eighth), closes the festival season. It is not strictly a Sukkot day — it is a separate appointed time, a kind of coda. The rabbis interpreted it as God saying to Israel: "Stay one more day. I am reluctant to see you go." After seven festivals, the sacred year ends not with a bang but with a lingering — a God who does not want the meeting to end.

### Verse 44: The Closing Summary

"So Moses announced to the Israelites the appointed times of the LORD." The chapter's closing verse is brief, but its simplicity carries weight. Moses does not merely write down the laws; he *announces* them. The mo'adim are proclaimed — they are public, communal, spoken aloud. The sacred calendar is not a private spiritual discipline; it is a community's shared life with God, announced and observed together. Moses is the voice; Israel is the congregation; the LORD is the host of every meeting on the calendar.

### The Theological Architecture of the Seven Feasts

The seven appointed times of Leviticus 23 form a complete theological arc when read together:

**Passover** (1st month, 14th day): The foundation — liberation from slavery. God acts to rescue his people. Time begins with grace.

**Unleavened Bread** (1st month, 15th–21st): The embodiment of liberation — living it, eating it, making it sensory. Seven days of enacted memory.

**Firstfruits** (first Sunday after Passover week): The consecration of the harvest — giving the first portion before taking any. A theology of dependence and gratitude.

**Shavuot/Pentecost** (fifty days after Firstfruits): The celebration of covenant and harvest — leavened loaves, comprehensive sacrifice, gleaning for the poor. Abundance consecrated and shared.

**Feast of Trumpets** (7th month, 1st day): The announcement — the shofar blast that opens the most sacred month. Preparation for judgment.

**Day of Atonement** (7th month, 10th day): The confrontation — Israel faces its failure before God and finds forgiveness through the high priest's mediation. The most solemn day of the year.

**Festival of Tabernacles** (7th month, 15th–22nd): The celebration of completed harvest and remembered wilderness — seven days of joyful, vulnerable living in temporary shelters. The year ends not with achievement but with gratitude and memory.

This is a complete human spiritual journey in compressed form: liberation, embodied memory, consecration, covenant celebration, self-examination, atonement, and grateful acknowledgment of dependence. The Hebrew year moves through every season of the soul.

### The New Testament Resonance

The appointed times of Leviticus 23 resonate through the New Testament in ways that are hard to overstate.

The New Testament presents Jesus's death as happening **at Passover** — the very moment of the lamb's sacrifice (John 18:28; 1 Cor 5:7: "Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed"). The Passover is not accidentally the backdrop; it is the lens through which the meaning of the crucifixion is understood. The liberation from Egypt becomes the template for liberation from sin.

The **Firstfruits** offering connects to Paul's language in 1 Corinthians 15:20, 23: "Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep." The resurrection is dated to the Sunday after Passover — the precise day of the wave offering. The risen Christ is the firstfruits of the harvest that will follow: the general resurrection of the dead. The wave offering of Leviticus 23 is waved again at Easter.

The **Day of Pentecost** in Acts 2 falls on the exact day of Shavuot — fifty days after Passover. The outpouring of the Spirit on the gathered disciples in Jerusalem is the fulfillment of the firstfruits-of-the-Spirit theology that Paul articulates in Romans 8:23 ("we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit"). The leavened loaves of Shavuot — real bread, made the ordinary way — anticipate the real people of the new community, brought before God as they are.

The **Day of Atonement** is extensively interpreted in Hebrews 9–10, where Jesus's death is understood as the ultimate Yom Kippur — the high priest entering the most holy place not with animal blood but with his own. The year's one entry into the holy of holies becomes the once-for-all entry that makes all subsequent entries unnecessary.

The **Festival of Tabernacles** resonates with John 1:14 ("the Word became flesh and *tabernacled* among us" — the Greek *eskēnōsen* is from *skēnē*, tent, booth, the same root as the Septuagint's translation of *sukkah*). God in the incarnation does what Israel did in the wilderness: takes up residence in a temporary structure, vulnerable and present, dwelling among the people.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 109–110)

Codex Vaticanus pages 109–110 preserve the Greek of Leviticus 23. The chapter opens at the top of page 109, marked with the chapter marker (ΚΖ — chapter 27 in the LXX's division of the text, which differs from the Hebrew chapter numbering). The Greek is legible and well-preserved across both pages.

The LXX translates *mo'ed* (appointed time, meeting) as *heortē* (feast, festival) in most instances, but also sometimes as *kairos* (the right time, the opportune moment — as distinguished from *chronos*, mere calendar time). The choice of *kairos* is theologically loaded: these are not merely scheduled dates but *opportune moments*, divinely appointed intersections between sacred and ordinary time. When the New Testament uses *kairos* to describe the "fullness of time" in which Christ came (Gal 4:4; Eph 1:10; Mark 1:15), it reaches back through the LXX into exactly this vocabulary of the mo'adim.

The key term *inui nefesh* (self-affliction for Yom Kippur, v.27) is rendered in the LXX as *tapeinōsete tas psychas hymōn* — "you shall humble your souls." The Greek *tapeinōsis* (humility, lowliness) is the word group that runs through Mary's *Magnificat* in Luke 1:48 ("he has regarded the *lowliness* of his servant") and through Paul's theology of Christ's self-emptying in Philippians 2:8 ("he humbled himself, becoming obedient even to death"). The Day of Atonement's demand for self-humbling is written in vocabulary that the New Testament picks up for the incarnation itself: the God who asks Israel to voluntarily humble themselves on Yom Kippur is the God who will, in Christ, voluntarily humble himself in the fullest possible act of self-abasement.

The command to "rejoice before the LORD" at Tabernacles (v.40) uses in the LXX the verb *euphrainthēsesthe* — from *euphraino*, to make joyful, to cheer. The LXX Psalms are saturated with this verb. The joy demanded at Sukkot is not a mild contentment; it is the full-throated, embodied, corporate joy of a people who know they have been brought through the wilderness and into the harvest. The *sukkah* does not dampen the joy; it grounds it. You rejoice most fully when you remember that the ground beneath your celebration is grace, not achievement.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 71v–72r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 109–110 (LXX).*
